text: Maximal independent set The maximal independent set (MIS) problem is a classical NP-hard problem. It is used in several applications and the MIS problem asks to compute a maximum independent set, i.e., a set of vertices of a maximal size on an undirected graph where no two different vertices in the set are adjacent. In the last years, exact methods, particularly branch-and-bound and branch-and-reduce algorithms, are among the most performed solvers. Adding new or new formulations of valid inequalities in the bounding algorithm (branch-and-bound), in combination with efficient reduction rules within the search strategy (branch-and-reduce), have greatly improved the solution times on almost all known benchmarks. Thanks to the new reduction rules, even very large real world instances can be solved optimally that were beyond reach before. It remains open what the optimal branching strategy within the branch-and-bound and branch-and-reduce framework is, and we are about to investigate an embedding of a GNN that performs better. Currently, the state of the art is to choose at random vertices based on their degree in each iteration. We here show a GNN that learns to compute which vertex should be chosen as a next branching vertex. The solvers are rather complex, which exclude simple supervised or reinforcement learning. Hence, as a proxy, we evolve parameters for a population-based genetic algorithm, leading to improved solution times on 73% of our benchmarks with median speedup of 24%.
explanation: The text shows a strong alignment with typical AI-generated research prose: polished but contains minor awkward phrasing and inconsistencies (e.g., 'maximal size' vs 'maximum', 'in the last years'), which are common in AI drafts yet less typical in a carefully edited human-authored abstract.
